results.title: Fugitive II
results.material_description: interactive software, video, motion controller, projection, computer, cameras and infra red flood lights
results.brief_description:
Fugitive II is the second iteration of the interactive digital video installation Fugitive (I), developed in 1996-7 at CMU, Pittsburgh USA and ZKM, Karlsruhe, Germany by Simon Penny, with the assistance of Jamie Schulte and Andre Bernhardt.

The question ‘what is Fugitive II’ may be answered from two directions. As a piece of interactive media research, Fugitive (I+II) attempt to move beyond a simplistic state-machine model of interactivity in which raw instantaneous location of the user triggers events. Fugitive responds to the ongoing behaviour of the user.

Fugitive (I+II) attempt to remove the levels of abstraction and encoding common to most interactive paradigms. Users interact in Fugitive with no special clothes or headgear, no input devices and no training. Fugitive responds to the natural embodied expression and dynamics of human users. It models a more convivial kind of human-computer interaction. Theoretically, Fugitive has much in common with situated, embodied and phenomenological critiques of conventional Artificial Intelligence approaches.

The imagery in Fugitive II speaks to the conflicted nostalgia of the expatriate. The movement of the user undoes the original cinematography: for instance, the moving projected image puts the frames of a pan back on the wall at angles relative to each other that correspond to the original arc of the camera. In so doing, FugitiveII falteringly, fragmentally, reconstructs a virtual recreation of a place, only to be disrupted, deconstructed, or to collapse, due to user movement. FugitiveII speaks to the impossibility and the folly of dwelling in memory.

results.title: Fly through visualisations
results.brief_description: Collection of nine fly throughs using 3D visualisations of the Cinemedia complex at Federation Square. Some show plaza , most move from plaza through to ground floor and escalators.
results.description: Collection of nine fly throughs using 3D visualisations of the Cinemedia complex at Federation Square. Some show plaza , most move from plaza through to ground floor and escalators.

results.title: Screen Gallery scaling visualisation
results.brief_description: Two visualisations. The first, the projected opening on May 9th, 2001 for the Cinemedia Screen Gallery. Includes 18 projectors, 16 plasma screens, 16 TFT screens and 24 TFT kiosks. The second on May 9th, 2003 includes 250 screens. Both show moving image clips projected onto different types and sizes of screens, and give a sense of how the screen gallery at Federation Sqaure will look and feel.

results.title: Just desserts
results.brief_description: This wonderfully sensuous short presents the rites of puberty as a menu of Italian favourites: a young girls first period occurs when she is stomping the grapes; gnocchi is forever to remind her of first having sex with a boy; burnt polenta is the taste of lesbian longing. A lovely remembrance of an Italo-Australian adolescence.

results.title: Chicks With Decks
results.brief_description: A documentary about teenage Australian girls with a love for skateboards and making rhymes. This student work features spectacular skateboarding footage.

results.title: Relative strangers
results.brief_description: A beautiful mediation on the meaning of family and blood ties. Photographs and home movies of the past form the extent of one man’s childhood memory of his father. In attempting to recover his past, he discovers that time’s absences and betrayals are apt to be repeated.

results.title: Joseph Schultz
results.brief_description: A young German soldier refuses to fire on a line of Yugoslav partisans. His disobedience costs him his life. Based on an actual event in the Second World War, Joseph Schultz uses both realistic reconstruction and documentary evidence to assert the continuing resonance of resistance.
